channel.title: The New Yorker — Reporting
channel.description: Latest reporting from The New Yorker

item.title: Could Switzerland Become the First Country to Cap Its Population?
item.description: The Swiss will soon go to the polls for a novel initiative that could upend the nation’s economy and rupture ties with the European Union.

item.title: Andrew Tate’s Empire of Abuse
item.description: How the defining figure of the manosphere built a fortune—and became a political force—by systematically exploiting women.

item.title: Misery Loves Company—If There Are Snacks
item.description: Do “Admin Nights” make people more productive or less lonely?

item.title: Ken Griffin’s Billions and Billions
item.description: The hedge-fund titan is an unabashed big spender—from pièds-a-terre to politics.

item.title: Inside the Ludicrous, Deadly Serious Plan to Take Over Greenland
item.description: “We want Greenland,” Trump said. Four men sprang into action to make fantasy a reality.

item.title: Jürgen Habermas Defended Reason in a Darkening Age
item.description: The great German philosopher, who died in March, understood how much depended on a principled public sphere.

item.title: The Repo Man Coming for Your Ride
item.description: As America’s auto debt nears $1.7 trillion, repossessions are reaching levels not seen since the Great Recession. Inside an industry at the front line of the country’s affordability crisis.

item.title: What Science Knows About Grief
item.description: After my husband’s death, I had never been more pliable, tender, open, or raw. It was then that I tried E.M.D.R. therapy.

item.title: The Teen Believers in a Christian America
item.description: For Charlie Kirk’s followers, faith and patriotism are intertwined.

item.title: Colson Whitehead’s Big Score
item.description: As he closes out his Harlem crime trilogy with “Cool Machine,” the two-time Pulitzer winner turns again to the city that made him, and to the private ghosts behind his restless reinventions.
